coredata.dc:title: Organic acid-induced triple fluorescent emission carbon quantum dots identification of distilled liquor
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jing
Deng, Jiaxi
Zheng, Jia
Ma, Yi
Luo, Huibo
Luo, Xiaogang
Hou, Changjun
Huo, Danqun
coredata.dc:description: An acid-sensitive carbon dot fluorescence sensing array was investigated for the differentiated recognition of distilled spirits. Due to the electrostatic interactions between CDs and organic acids, organic acids affect the protonation and fluorescence properties of CDs, which in turn modify the CDs triple fluorescence emission. The regular linear variation of the fluorescence sensor was found under acidic conditions (3.0 < pH < 6.2). A comprehensive study of acids with selectivity, different concentrations (0.1 mM, 1 mM, 10 mM, 40 mM), different types (8 species) and mixed acids (formic acid and acetic acid), and good quantification capability for acetic acid (0.01–1 mM). Demonstrating good recognition ability of the sensor array for complex analyte. On this basis, the fluorescence sensor array was applied to the classification and recognition of liquors. LDA has realized the identification of 16 kinds of Baijiu and 21 kinds of distilled liquors with an accuracy of 100%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Distilled liquor
Organic acids
Acid-sensitive quantum dots
Fluorescence sensor array

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principal of the fluorescence sensor array

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of CDs fluorescence sensor array for distilled liquor detection.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs when reacting with different concentrations of acetic acid. (B) The scatter diagram of F360/F292 and acetic acid concentrations. (C) The linear relationship between F360/F292 and acetic acid concentrations.
Detection of organic acids. (A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs when reacting with eight kinds of organic acids. (B) Fingerprint map of organic acids. (C) Heat map of organic acids.
Detection of sixteen kinds of Baijiu. (A) Fingerprint map of Baijiu. (B) Heat map of Baijiu. (C, D) Discrimination of sixteen kinds of Baijiu.
Binding constant of CDs and organic acids.
Comparison of the presented study with some published methods.

head.ce:title: Organic acid-induced triple fluorescent emission carbon quantum dots identification of distilled liquor

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors made equal contribution to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An acid-sensitive carbon dot fluorescence sensing array was investigated for the differentiated recognition of distilled spirits. Due to the electrostatic interactions between CDs and organic acids, organic acids affect the protonation and fluorescence properties of CDs, which in turn modify the CDs triple fluorescence emission. The regular linear variation of the fluorescence sensor was found under acidic conditions (3.0 < pH < 6.2). A comprehensive study of acids with selectivity, different concentrations (0.1 mM, 1 mM, 10 mM, 40 mM), different types (8 species) and mixed acids (formic acid and acetic acid), and good quantification capability for acetic acid (0.01–1 mM). Demonstrating good recognition ability of the sensor array for complex analyte. On this basis, the fluorescence sensor array was applied to the classification and recognition of liquors. LDA has realized the identification of 16 kinds of Baijiu and 21 kinds of distilled liquors with an accuracy of 100%.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Brandy, whisky, vodka, gin, rum and Baijiu are six major distilled liquors in the world. Among them, Baijiu is made from a complex process of steaming, saccharification, fermentation and distillation by using grain as main raw material and Daqu, Xiaoqu and yeast as saccharifying agents (Sakandar, Hussain, Farid Khan, & Zhang, 2020; Song et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022). Compared with other distilled liquors, Baijiu exhibited unparalleled flavor and taste, which gains maximum benefit from the unmatched brewing techniques of koji making and solid-state fermentation (Pang et al., 2020), as well as the geographical factor endowed by nature. The content of flavor substances in Baijiu far exceeds that of other distilled spirits. Up to now, more than 1700 kinds of flavor components including alcohols, aldehydes, acids and esters have been detected in Baijiu (Xu et al., 2022), possessing different physicochemical properties such as acidity, volatility, polarity, solubility and thermal stability.

Organic acids, mainly including acetic acid, propionic acid, butyric acid, capric acid and lactic acid, are important flavor components in distilled liquors, affecting the flavor presentation (Wei, Zou, & Shen, 2020). Brandy is the only one of the world's top six distilled liquors that extracts organic acids from grape. In the case of other five distilled liquors, the produce of organic acid mainly come from the fermentation of bacteria. The liquor industry has undergone rapid development with the mutual integration of modern technology and traditional brewing methods (Niu, Zhu, & Xiao, 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Nevertheless, some issues have also been exposed, such as random provisioning, indiscriminate labeling and substandard quality, severely disturbing the regular order of liquor industry. In order to maintain the brand image, the development of liquor identification technology is all the more essential.

At present, the common methods for distilled liquor detection mainly rely on sensory evaluation and instrumental analysis (He et al., 2020). Sensory evaluation can bring distilled spirits comprehensive judgment and instrumental analysis can achieve the qualitative and quantitative detection of analytes, both playing important roles for quality control of Baijiu (Jia, Du, Fan, Wang, & Shi, 2022). Nonetheless, the application scope of sensory evaluation is hard to expand as it requires professional tasters and comfortable environment (Hu, Qiu, Dai, Tian, & Wei, 2022). Although large equipment is constantly improving and the accurate analysis of flavor components can be achieved (Wang et al., 2022), only single or a few analytes could be detected simultaneously (Jia et al., 2020), which cannot reflect the overall information of liquor (He et al., 2022). Considering the problems above, a simple and quick method for distilled liquor detection is clamant.

In recent years, sensor array has been fast developed as a powerful method for the detection of complex analytes and applied in the broad fields of Baijiu (Liu et al., 2022), food (Ma et al., 2022), pesticide (Jiang, Chattopadhyay, Geng, & Rotello, 2022) and metal ions (Wang et al., 2021). The sensor array consists of a series of sensitive elements that can react with multiple analytes in different degrees (Kim et al., 2020; Wang, Fang, Wu, Zhang, & Shi, 2020). For each analyte, a unique fingerprint map could be formed by cross-reaction (Cao et al., 2020; Hou, Fan, Fan, Liang, Zhang, & Ding, 2021). The identification of analytes can be achieved by pattern recognition. Compared to sensors for a single analyte, simultaneous detection of multiple analytes can be achieved by cross-response, although sensor array may not be sensitive or selective enough (Li, Xu, Yuan, & Zhang, 2022; Zhang & Tan, 2022).

Carbon dots, a kind of promising fluorescent nanomaterial with a size of less than 10 nm (Ansi & Renuka, 2019a), possessing the advantages of simple preparation (Zhang et al., 2019), large Stokes shift, wide excitation and narrow emission range (Liu, Zhang, Hu, Gao, & Zhang, 2020), stable optical performance and long fluorescence lifetime (Huang et al., 2020), have been widely applied in the field of analysis detection (Wu et al., 2014). Renuka prepared PL gel by mixing CDs and Gum Arabic. The luminescent marker exhibited considerable adherence over a large amount of substrates and presented a wide pH detection range of 2–10 (Ansi & Renuka, 2019b). Zhang synthesized CDs via microwave irradiation by using kelp and ethylenediamine, exhibiting high sensitivity toward pH and Co2+ (Zhao, Li, Cheng, & Yang, 2019).

Before this investigation, although CDs have been employed for the detection of organic acids or Baijiu, they still suffer from poor selectivity and the need for other materials to be engaged. Here, acid-sensitive CDs with triple emission were prepared and applied directly for the classification of organic acids and distilled liquors. The fluorescence intensity of CDs can be enhanced or quenched owing to the reaction of organic acids and CDs. The characteristic fingerprint map can be generated by cross-response. Thus, different analytes can be identified by pattern recognition. Using the fluorescence sensor array, 8 kinds of organic acids, different ratios of formic acid-acetic acid mixtures, 16 kinds of Baijiu and five other distilled liquors were identified.

All reagents applied in the assay were at least analytical grade without further purification. Formic acid, acetic acid, propionic acid, butyric acid, valerate acid, hexanoic acid, heptanoic acid, lactic acid, o-phenylenediamine, chitosan, citric acid and sodium citrate were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). The different brands and flavor types of Baijiu were obtained from a supermarket. The details of organic acids and Baijiu were shown in Table S1 and Table S2.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL Ltd., Japan) was applied for the morphology characterization of CDs. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of CDs were recorded by a TU-1901 double-beam spectrophotometer (Peking General Instrument Co. Ltd., Beijing, China). The fluorescence spectra was measured by a FL970 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Tianmei Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). FTIR was measured by Nicolet iS50 (Thermo, USA). XPS was characterized by ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo, USA). Quantum yields and fluorescence lifetimes were tested by FLS1000 transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Raman spectra were measured by microscopic confocal Raman spectroscopy (HORIBA Jobin Yvon S.A.S, France). The deionized water used in all assays was prepared by an 18.2 MΩ Millipore Direct-Q water system (Molsheim, France) (Table S3).

The preparation of CDs was based on the previous report with appropriate modifications (Xu et al., 2020). First, 0.1 g OPD and 0.1 g chitosan were dissolved into 10 mL water. Then, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL autoclave reactor and heated at 180 ℃ for 10 h. Finally, the product was cooled to room temperature and filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The obtained CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃.

The solution with different pH values (3.0, 3.4, 3.8, 4.2, 4.6, 5.0, 5.4, 5.8, 6.2, 6.6) was prepared by mixing the solution of citric acid and sodium citrate. To research the influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, 50 μL solution of citric acid-sodium citrate and 50 μL CDs were introduced into 400 μL water. After 10 min of incubation, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was measured by the fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Different concentrations of acetic acid (0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50 mM) were detected and the test method was consistent with the detection of pH. The identification of different organic acids was conducted as follows: Take 0.1 mM organic acids as an example. In a 500 μL centrifuge tube, 50 μL organic acids and 50 μL CDs were introduced into 400 μL water. The fluorescence intensity of CDs at 292, 360 and 578 nm was measured by the fluorescence spectrophotometer after 10 min of incubation. Each organic acid was tested 5 times to give a raw data matrix of 8 analytes × 3 channels × 5 replicates. HCA and LDA were applied for statistical analysis. In addition, other three concentrations of organic acids (1, 10, and 40 mM) were also detected and the way was the same as that of 0.1 mM.

The six major distilled spirits of the world were tested, but because liquor has more aromatic flavors, 16 brands of Baijiu were tested even further. The operation procedure was the same as that of different organic acids. The distilled liquor were repeatedly measured 5 times to generate the raw data matrix of 16 analytes × 3 channels × 5 replicates.

The prepared CDs was characterized by TEM, fluorescence spectroscopy, XPS and FTIR, respectively. As the TEM image shown in Fig. S1A, the CDs exhibited fine dispersion. The particle size distribution of CDs (Fig. S1B) was fitted by a Gaussian function, and the approximate mean particle size of 2.71 nm was obtained. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs were shown in Fig. S1 C. When excited at 255 nm, CDs presented triple fluorescence emissions at 292, 360, and 578 nm. CDs appeared light yellow under 365 nm UV lamp.

The XPS characterization of CDs was shown in Fig. S2 . In the full spectrum, three characteristic peaks appeared at 286, 400 and 533 eV, corresponding to C1 s, N1 s and O1 s, respectively (Fig. S2 A). In the high-resolution spectrum of C1 s (Fig. S2 B), 285.1, 286.7 and 288.6 eV correspond to CN, CO and CO bonds, respectively. N1 s was also divided into three peaks (Fig. S2 C), which locating at 398.8, 399.9 and 401.4 eV, corresponding to pyridine nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen in turn. As the high-resolution spectrum of O1 s shown in Fig. S2 D, C O and CO were located at 532.4 and 533.7 eV, separately. The functional groups of CDs were further measured by FTIR. In Fig. S2E, the characteristic peak located at 3351 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of OH/NH. The characteristic peak of 1620 cm−1 can be attributed to the vibrations of the amide group. The peaks near 1503, 1409 and 1109 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CC, CN and CO, respectively. The results above indicated the successful synthesis of CDs.

The fluorescence lifetime of CDs (Fig. S3A) was calculated to be 5.94 ns from the fluorescence decay curve, which was in accordance with the double exponential function. The quantum yield of CDs was measured 3.32% by referring the method of Guo (Fig. S3 B) (Guo et al., 2022). Raman spectroscopy was used to further characterize the structure of the CDs. From Fig. S3C, two peaks appear at 1368 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1, representing the D peak of C atom defects and the G peak of sp2 hybridization, respectively, and IG/ID = 1.05 was calculated on the data, which indicates that the material was disordered or had more defective areas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principal of the fluorescence sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, acid-sensitive CDs synthesized by o-phenylenediamine and chitosan was applied to construct fluorescence sensor array for the discrimination of organic acids and distilled liquor (Fig. 1 ). The specific recognition performance to organic acids mainly ascribe to the interaction of organic acids and the surface functional groups of CDs. Acid-sensitive CDs exhibited triple fluorescence emissions at 292, 360 and 578 nm. Organic acids could bind to CDs through electrostatic interaction, which affects the protonation and changes the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The protonation degree of the CDs is influenced by the type of organic acids. Organic acids with different pKa presented various of protonation level to CDs. The strongly acidic organic acids with low pKa can promote the protonation of amino group and induce the deprotonate of carboxyl group on the surface of CDs. Thus, the fluorescence emission centers from edge states of CDs would be buried by electrostatic interaction, leading fluorescence quench of the CDs.

To research the interaction ability of CDs and organic acids, the fluorescence intensity of CDs before and after reacting with organic acids was brought into the Stern-Volmer formula to calculate the binding constant of CDs and organic acids. As the results shown in Table 1 , the binding constant of different organic acids and CDs were significantly different. Some correlation existed between the binding constant and pKa, the lower the pKa of organic acid, the higher the binding constant of organic acid and CDs. The binding constant of CDs and organic acids were calculated according to the Stern-Volmer formula (Eq. (1)):
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 + K s v
[ M ]
F0 and F were CDs fluorescence intensity before and after reacting with organic acids. Ksv was a constant. M was the concentration of organic acids.

The triple channels fluorescence signal of CDs is helpful for the construction of the sensor array. When reacting with organic acids or liquor, the fluorescence intensity of CDs could be regulated in varying degrees owing to the difference of acidity and pKa of organic acids. For each analyte, a specific fingerprint map would be generated. The identification of organic acids or distilled liquor will be achieved by pattern recognition.

The influence of pH on the fluorescence performance of CDs was investigated. As the fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. S4 A, as the increase of pH from 3.0 to 6.6, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 292 nm increased continuously. On the contrary, the fluorescence emission peak at 360 nm decreased gradually. F360 (the fluorescence intensity at 360 nm) exhibited a good linear relationship with pH value in the range of 3.0–6.2. The linear equation and R2 were Y = −3618.4 X + 23940 and 0.9942, respectively (Fig. S4 B).

Different concentrations of acetic acid (0.01–50 mM) were detected to research the quantitative analysis ability of the CDs towards organic acids. The results were shown in Fig. 2 . As the increase of acetic acid, the fluorescence intensity at 292 nm decreased continuously, while increased gradually at 580 nm. As the scatter diagram of F360/F292 (ratio of fluorescence intensity at 360 and 292 nm) against the concentration of acetic acid shown in Fig. 2 B, with the increase of acetic acid, F360/F292 kept increasing and gradually became stable. F360/F292 presented a good linear relationship with the concentration of acetic acid in the range of 0.01–1 mM. The linear equation and R2 were Y = 0.9961 X + 0.0792 and 0.9945, respectively (Fig. 2 C).

LDA, HCA and PCA were further applied for the identification of different concentration of acetic acid and the results were shown in Fig. S5 . In HCA ( Fig. S5
A), samples of the same concentration were first gathered into one group and samples from different concentrations were connected in turn according to the distance. In LDA diagram, all samples were regularly distributed from left to right according to the concentration and five parallel samples of the same concentration were grouped into one cluster ( Fig. S5
B). In Fig. S5
C, the PCA plot showed dispersion of organic acids among the parallel samples, but a clear separation of the different concentrations was obtained. The results above demonstrated good quantitative detection property of the CDs towards acetic acid.

Considering the property that CDs was sensitive to acetic acid and pH, different kinds of organic acids (1 mM) were tested to assess the discrimination ability of the CDs-based sensor array. As the results shown in Fig. 3
A, CDs presented entirely different fluorescence intensity at wavelengths of 292, 360 and 578 nm when reacting with eight organic acids, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of CDs could be affected by the species of organic acids. With the increase of organic acids molecular weight, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually increased at 292 and 578 nm, and conversely, decreased at 360 nm except for acetic acid and lactic acid. It was interesting that CDs exhibited similar fluorescence intensity when reacting with formic acid and lactic acid, which may be caused by the similar acidic properties of these two organic acids. The fingerprint map and heat map of organic acids were also carried out to reflect the influence of organic acids on CDs intuitively. For each organic acid, a unique fingerprint and color could be generated (Fig. 3 B and 3C).

The effect of inorganic acids on CD was further investigated ( Fig. S6 ). Five representative inorganic acids (hydrochloric acids, sulfuric acids, nitric acids, phosphoric acids and boric acids, 0.1 mM) were chosen to cover strong, medium and weak inorganic acids. As seen from Fig. S6
A, while sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, and nitric acid are all strong acids, sulfuric acid can ionize twice as much H+ at the same molar concentration, making it more acidic than hydrochloric acid and nitric acid. Since hydrochloric acid and nitric acid are similar in acidity, their fluorescence intensity overlapped. When phosphoric acids introduced, the fluorescence intensity at 292 nm was higher than that of strong acids, and the fluorescence intensity at 360 nm was lower than that of strong acids. Boric acid, as a kind of weak inorganic acid, has a unique ionization mechanism. When water ionizes H+ and OH–, the empty orbitals of boric acid combine with the lone electrons of OH–, leaving the H+. As can be seen from both the fluorescence spectra ( Fig. S6
A) and fluorescence intensity histograms ( Fig. S6
B), with the acidity enhancement of inorganic acids, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased at 292 nm and 578 nm, and increased at 360 nm.

The mechanism of CDs was further investigated by reacting with acid and alkali. As shown in Fig. S6
C, when hydrochloric acid mixed with CDs, a dramatic change in fluorescence pattern presented. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity at 292 nm and 578 nm abruptly decreased, while the fluorescence intensity at 360 nm appeared to be enhanced. When sodium hydroxide was further introduced, it can be seen that the fluorescence spectrum was close to the original state of CDs. The reversible fluorescence change of CDs may be caused by the protonation and deprotonation process of CDs.

In addition, eight kinds of organic acids with different concentrations (0.1, 1, 10, and 40 mM) were tested to explore the recognition ability of the sensor array for organic acids. The canonical scores of the organic acids for the first two functions were plotted in Fig. S7 . At a certain concentration, different organic acids could be accurately distinguished. 40 data points (8 organic acids × 5 replicates) were grouped into 8 clusters, which correspond to 8 kinds of organic acids. Different organic acids were separated from each other, suggesting good recognition properties of the sensor array.

After successful identification of organic acids at the same concentration, organic acids (acetic acid, propionic acid and valeric acid) at different concentrations (1, 10 and 40 mM) were further carried out. As the results presented in Fig. S8 , the analytes with low concentration (1 mM) were significantly kept far away from the samples with medium (10 mM) and high (40 mM) concentration. While in the enlarged figure, the samples with medium and high concentration can be also accurately identified. This may be caused by the fact that organic acids with medium and high concentration exhibited relatively great impact on the fluorescence performance of, while they cannot further affect CDs fluorescence intensity when the analytes concentration exceed 10 mM.

The successful identification of diverse organic acids proved that the CDs-based sensor array had excellent recognition performance for single analyte. To further explore the detection ability of the sensor array to mixture samples, the binary organic acids of formic acid and acetic acid with different volume ratios (100%+0%, 90%+10%, 80%+20%, 70%+30%, 60%+40%, 50%+50%, 40%+60%, 30%+70%, 20%+80%, 10%+90%, 0%+100%) were measured. In HCA diagram ( Fig. S9
A), according to the distance of samples, five parallel samples of the same ratio were first classified separately and then samples from different ratio were connected. As the LDA plot shown in Fig. S9
B, all samples were divided into 11 clusters and were regularly distributed from left to right according to volume ratio, demonstrating good recognition ability of the sensor array for mixture analytes.

Selectivity is a vital index to evaluate the sensing performance of CDs, and is the key factor whether CDs can be applied for actual samples research. Thus, the selectivity assay of CDs was carried out to verify the specific detection ability of the sensor array for organic acids. The concentration of organic acids was 1 mM and other components (alcohols, esters and aldehydes) was 100 times that of organic acids. As the results shown in Fig. S10 , compared with control group (water), the fluorescence intensity of CDs was significantly reduced at 292 and 578 nm, while increased at 360 nm when reacting with formic acid, acetate acid and propionic acid. On the contrary, when alcohols, esters and aldehydes were introduced, the fluorescence signal of CDs changed slightly, indicating that these analytes could not react with CDs and their influence on the fluorescence performance of CDs could be ignored. These results indicated the good selectivity of the CDs towards organic acids and CDs could be applied for the detection of organic acids in real samples.

In the process of Baijiu production, apart from a large amount of ethanol and water, alcohols, acids, aldehydes and esters were also produced by microbial fermentation. Organic acids are important components in Baijiu, which is fermented by acetic acid bacteria and lactic acid bacteria, mainly including acetic acid, propionic acid and butyric acid, contributing greatly to the flavor and taste of Baijiu, determining the quality of products. According to the difference of raw material and technique, as well as the microbial community structure, the species and contents of organic acids in Baijiu were different from each other. The 16 kinds of Baijiu used in assay are produced in different provinces or regions in China, covering more than 10 kinds of flavor types such as strong fragrance, sauce fragrance, and light fragrance. Therefore, based on the difference of organic acids, different Baijiu samples were further discriminated to explore the practical application ability of the sensor array.

As the fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. 4 , CDs exhibited various fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 292, 360 and 578 nm when reacting with different Baijiu. The difference among Baijiu was also presented by the fingerprint map and heat map (Fig. 4 A and 4B), from which the difference was intuitively reflected and the preliminary identification of Baijiu could be achieved. In addition, LDA was further employed to evaluate the recognition ability of the sensor array. As the results shown in Fig. 4 C and 4D, all Baijiu samples were divided into 16 clusters, and five parallel samples of the same Baijiu were collected into one cluster.

Based on the successful identification of Baijiu, five distilled liquors of rum, vodka, gin, brandy and whisky were further tested to explored the application prospect of the fluorescent sensor array on distilled liquors from different countries. As the LDA scatter plot shown in Figs. S11, S21 distilled liquors can be accurately classified.

The comparison of present study and previous works was listed in Table 2 . The number of materials, array points and samples among different methods was systematically analyzed. It can be seen that the present sensor array, with a small number of sensing elements and array points, can detect 21 kinds of distilled liquors simultaneously. Not only does it conserve resources, it is also more convenient for operation and data processing. A low-cost, sensitive and easy-to-use sensor array has been created.

In this work, triple emission acid-sensitive CDs was prepared and applied to construct fluorescence sensor array for the recognition of acids and distilled liquors. Due to the electrostatic interaction between CDs and organic acids, the fluorescence intensity of CDs showed different degrees of enhancement or quenching. In the acidic environment of pH 3.0–6.2, F360 showed a good linear relationship with pH, and the linear equation was Y = −3618.4 X + 23940. Meanwhile, F360/F292 had a good linear relationship with acetic acid concentration in the range of 0.01–1 mM, with the linear equation of Y = 0.9961 X + 0.0792. Based on cross-response, the fluorescent sensor array was successfully applied for identification of 8 kinds of organic acids at 4 different concentrations (0.1 mM, 1 mM, 10 mM, 40 mM). The mixture of formic acid and acetic acid with different ratios were also accurately distinguished, indicating the excellent detection performance of the sensor array for mixed samples. In addition, the fluorescence sensor array was applied for the identification of different brands of distilled liquors. The accurate identification of 16 kinds of Baijiu was achieved. Notably, rum, vodka, gin, brandy and whisky were further tested and 21 kinds of distilled liquors were also successfully identified. We hope that the method will provide a new strategy for Baijiu identification and quality control.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the Sichuan Science and Technology Program [grant number 21ZYZFZDYF0019], Brew Microorganisms Technology and Application of Key Laboratory Project in Sichuan Province [grant number NJ2020-03], Key Laboratory of Wuliangye-flavor Liquor Solid-state Fermentation, China National Light Industry [grant number 2021JJ001], Key Laboratory of Brewing Molecular Engineering of China Light Industry [grant number BME-202108], Wuliangye Group research project [grant number CXY2020ZR004], Chongqing Graduate Tutor Team Construction Project, Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University for (the characterization of TEM/XPS/FT-IR) and the sharing fund of Chongqing University's large equipment.

Changjun Hou, Danqun Huo and Xiaogang Luo contributed to the conception of the study; Jing Zhang, Jiaxi Deng, per-formed the experiments; Jing Zhang performed the data analyses and wrote the manuscript; Jia Zheng, Yi Ma, and Huibo Luo helped to perform the analysis with constructive discussions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136080.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
